summary: French Enlightenment philosopher and author, known for satirizing Leibniz's optimism in Candide, writing the Philosophical Dictionary and Letters on the English, and advocating tolerance. Famous for the quote 'if God did not exist, it would be necessary to invent him' and the phrase 'crush the infamous.'

works.name: Candide and Leibniz Satire
works.description: Voltaire's most famous literary work, Candide, is a satire of Leibniz's Theodicy via the character Pangloss, who insists we live in the 'best of all possible worlds.' Clues frequently reference Pangloss rationalizing disasters like syphilis and the Lisbon earthquake, and the novel's concluding injunction to 'cultivate our garden.' This is the most common giveaway identifier for Voltaire in quizbowl.

clues.clue: Satirized Leibniz's belief that we live in the 'best of all possible worlds' via the character Pangloss in Candide
examples: what author parodied Leibniz's Theodicy via the character Pangloss in his novel Candide?
Du Chatelet frequently corresponded with this thinker, who satirized Leibniz as Doctor Pangloss in Candide

clues.clue: Candide's conclusion urges that we 'must cultivate our garden'

clues.clue: Pangloss rationalizes syphilis and the Lisbon earthquake
examples: created a character who rationalizes his syphilis and the Lisbon earthquake

clues.clue: Contra Leibniz, claimed we live in the worst of all possible worlds (attributed via Candide's satire)
examples: claimed that, contra Leibniz, we live in the worst of all possible worlds

works.name: Philosophical Letters (Letters on the English)
works.description: The Philosophical Letters, also known as Letters on the English or Letters Concerning the English Nation, discuss topics including the Quakers, Isaac Newton, and the trial of Charles I. They praise Francis Bacon as the 'father of experimental philosophy' and also address the problem of evil, including a rejection of Pascal's theological approach. Voltaire had to flee Paris after publishing them, as they praised inoculation and attacked French religion.

works.name: Philosophical Dictionary
works.description: The Philosophical Dictionary (Dictionnaire philosophique portatif) was published anonymously in Geneva by Gabriel Grasset. It has a similar form to a work by Bayle, and outlines virtue via a dialogue between 'The Honest Man' and 'The Excrement' after telling the story of Brutus committing suicide. In the Dictionary, Voltaire argues against the Judeo-Christian conception of God but affirms an impersonal deity, thus espousing deism.

clues.clue: Published anonymously in Geneva by Gabriel Grasset; similar form to Bayle's work
examples: Gabriel Grassset published this work in Geneva to maintain its author's anonymity

examples: outlines virtue via a dialogue between 'The Honest Man' and 'The Excrement' after telling the story of Brutus committing suicide

clues.clue: Argues against Judeo-Christian God but affirms an impersonal deity (deism)
examples: Voltaire argued that the Judeo-Christian conception of God was false, but that there was an impersonal deity

clues.clue: Often paired with his entries for the Encyclopedie edited by Diderot

works.name: Treatise on Tolerance
works.description: Voltaire's Treatise on Tolerance (Traite sur la Tolerance) uses the case of Jean Calas, a Calvinist who was innocent but killed on the wheel, as evidence that all men should be brothers. This work is frequently clued alongside his broader attacks on religious dogmatism.

clues.clue: Used the example of Jean Calas as evidence for tolerance; Calas was an innocent Calvinist killed on the wheel

examples: his Treatise on Tolerance

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: General identifiers for Voltaire include being a French Enlightenment philosopher, his famous quote 'if God did not exist, it would be necessary to invent him,' his call to 'crush the infamous' (ecrasez l'infame), his relationship with Emilie du Chatelet, his correspondence with Frederick the Great, and his imprisonment in the Bastille. He also argued that China had 'perfected moral science' and wrote on social history.

comprehensive_summary:
Voltaire (Francois-Marie Arouet) appears in quizbowl as one of the central figures of the French Enlightenment. His most common giveaway is as the author of Candide, a satire of Leibniz's Theodicy via the character Pangloss, who maintains that we live in the 'best of all possible worlds' despite catastrophes like syphilis and the Lisbon earthquake. The novel concludes with the injunction to 'cultivate our garden.'

His Philosophical Letters (Letters on the English) are the next most frequently clued work. They discuss the Quakers, praise Isaac Newton's rational method (popularizing the apple myth) over Descartes, address the problem of evil (rejecting Pascal's approach in the 25th letter), and praise Francis Bacon as the 'father of experimental philosophy.' Their publication forced Voltaire to flee Paris because they praised inoculation and attacked French religion.

The Philosophical Dictionary, published anonymously in Geneva by Gabriel Grasset, is structured similarly to Bayle's dictionary and argues for deism against the Judeo-Christian God. It contains the dialogue between 'The Honest Man' and 'The Excrement.' The Treatise on Tolerance uses the case of Jean Calas, an innocent Calvinist killed on the wheel, to argue for religious tolerance.

Key biographical identifiers include: the famous quote 'if God did not exist, it would be necessary to invent him' (from his commentary on The Three Imposters, inverted by Bakunin); the rallying cry 'crush the infamous' (ecrasez l'infame); his relationship with Emilie du Chatelet, who translated Newton's Principia; his correspondence with Frederick the Great; his imprisonment in the Bastille; and his argument that China had 'perfected moral science.' David Friedrich Strauss published lectures on Voltaire, and Voltaire's essay on universal history is credited with founding social history.

Work: discusses the Quakers, Isaac Newton, and the trial of Charles I; its author had to flee Paris after praising inoculation
cards.back: Philosophical Letters / Letters on the English (Voltaire)

Philosopher: Mikhail Bakunin inverted this thinker's famous quote to say 'If God really existed, it would be necessary to abolish him'
Philosopher: imagines the 'obstinate pride' needed to say 'only my anthill is beloved of God' among 'nine hundred million little ants'
Philosopher: argued that China had 'perfected moral science' and advocated integrating Chinese social structures into Europe
